intro:
### TL;DR (read this if nothing else)
- **95-99% of your gradient signal is getting crushed** by the LM head before it reaches most of your model's parameters. This might be the biggest training inefficiency nobody talks about.
- A new RL trick fuses a pretrained value model with tiny rollout batches, beating GRPO/DAPO by 10%+ on math while using group size *4*. Your GPU bill just got smaller.
- Instead of teaching embeddings what text *means*, teach them what the LLM would *say* about it. New MTEB SOTA, no labels needed, and it even reduces toxic retrieval by 43%.

---
footer:
## 🌡️ Field Pulse
The gradient bottleneck paper feels like one of those "how did nobody formalize this before" moments. If it holds up, expect a wave of papers on alternative LM head architectures in the next few months. Meanwhile, RL efficiency is the hottest arms race right now. Between V₀.₅ and ICRL, the message is clear: brute-force rollouts are out, clever priors and curriculum tricks are in. The embedding space is also quietly getting interesting again with generative approaches challenging the contrastive learning orthodoxy.
---

papers.title: Your LLM's Last Layer Is Eating Its Own Homework
papers.water_cooler: Did you see that paper showing 95-99% of gradient signal dies in the LM head? Makes you wonder how much better models would be if we just... fixed that layer.
papers.markdown:
**Actual title:** Lost in Backpropagation: The LM Head is a Gradient Bottleneck | **From:** Nathan Godey et al. | [arxiv.org/abs/2603.10145](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10145)

**One-liner:** The output projection layer is silently destroying almost all your gradient signal during backprop.

**The problem:** Everyone knows about the softmax bottleneck (projecting D-dim features to V-dim vocabulary where D << V limits expressivity). Turns out it's not just an expressivity problem. It's an optimization disaster.

**The trick:** You know how backprop sends gradients backward through each layer? When a V-dimensional gradient passes back through a rank-D matrix, it gets crushed into a D-dimensional subspace. That's unavoidable linear algebra. The result: 95-99% of the gradient norm just vanishes before it reaches the rest of your model. Every layer below the LM head is training on a severely distorted signal.

**Numbers:** Controlled pretraining experiments show the bottleneck makes trivially learnable patterns *unlearnable* and fundamentally warps training dynamics. This is architecture-agnostic, so it hits Llama and GPT alike.

**The catch:** They diagnose the disease thoroughly but only gesture at cures. No concrete new LM head design that fixes it (yet).

papers.title: The Value Model Whisperer: RL on a Budget
papers.vibe_label: Really solid, bookmark for the weekend
papers.water_cooler: Did you see V₀.₅? They're getting better RL results with 4 rollouts than GRPO gets with full groups, by using a pretrained value model as a prior.
papers.markdown:
**Actual title:** V₀.₅: Generalist Value Model as Prior for Sparse RL Rollouts | **From:** Yi-Kai Zhang et al. | [arxiv.org/abs/2603.10848](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10848)

**One-liner:** Pretrained value model + 4 rollouts = better than GRPO with way more samples.

**The problem:** RLVR methods like GRPO need lots of rollouts to build a decent advantage baseline. More rollouts = more compute = more pain. But if you use too few, your variance explodes and training goes sideways.

**The trick:** What if you already had a pretty good guess at the value before rolling out? V₀.₅ takes a pretrained "generalist value model" (V₀) as a Bayesian prior, then fuses it with the empirical mean from just a handful of rollouts. A real-time statistical test checks whether the prior is trustworthy for each batch. If it's hallucinating, the system automatically allocates more rollout budget. Think of it as "trust but verify" for value estimation.

**Numbers:** 10%+ over GRPO and DAPO across six math reasoning benchmarks, with group size 4. That's absurdly efficient. Faster convergence too.

**The catch:** The pretrained value model itself needs to be good. If your domain is weird enough that V₀ has no useful prior, you're back to square one.

papers.title: Embeddings That Think Before They Embed
papers.vibe_label: Really solid, bookmark for the weekend
papers.water_cooler: Did you see that paper where they make embeddings by predicting what the LLM would say instead of encoding what the text means? Beat MTEB SOTA without any labeled data.
papers.markdown:
**Actual title:** LLM2Vec-Gen: Generative Embeddings from Large Language Models | **From:** Parishad BehnamGhader et al. | [arxiv.org/abs/2603.10913](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.10913)

**One-liner:** Instead of embedding what text says, embed what the LLM would reply. New unsupervised MTEB king.

**The problem:** Text embedding models encode input semantics, but embedding tasks need to map diverse inputs to similar outputs. Contrastive learning with paired data is the standard fix, but good pairs are expensive.

**The trick:** Add special trainable tokens to the LLM vocabulary. Append them to input. Train those tokens to represent what the LLM *would generate* as a response, compressed into a fixed-length embedding. The LLM backbone stays frozen. Training uses only unlabeled queries plus distillation from an unsupervised teacher. Because you're capturing the model's generative intent, you inherit its reasoning ability and safety alignment for free.

**Numbers:** +9.3% over the best unsupervised method on MTEB. 43.2% less harmful content retrieved. 29.3% better reasoning on embedding tasks. The embeddings are even decodable back to text, so you can actually read what they "mean."

**The catch:** Frozen backbone means you're capped by whatever the base LLM knows. Also, the decodable-to-text property is cool but unclear how useful in production.

papers.title: SFT Is Dead, Long Live Few-Shot RL
papers.vibe_label: Interesting if you're in this subfield
papers.water_cooler: Did you see that ICRL paper? They completely skip SFT for tool use, just do RL with fading few-shot examples. Labeled data for tool training might be over.
papers.markdown:
**Actual title:** ICRL: In-Context Reinforcement Learning for Tool Use | **From:** Yiran Zhao et al. | [arxiv.org/abs/2603.08068](https://arxiv.org/abs/2603.08068)

**One-liner:** Skip SFT entirely. Just show the model a few tool-use examples during RL rollouts, then gradually take the training wheels off.

**The problem:** Teaching LLMs to use tools typically requires an SFT warmup phase with expensive labeled demonstrations before you can do RL. That's a data bottleneck and a pipeline headache.

**The trick:** During RL rollouts, stuff a few in-context examples of correct tool calls into the prompt. The model learns the format from examples while RL teaches it *when* to use tools. As training progresses, you fade out the examples until the model operates zero-shot. It's basically curriculum learning meets in-context learning, but for RL.

**Numbers:** SOTA across reasoning and tool-use benchmarks, matching or beating SFT+RL pipelines while needing zero labeled demonstration data.

**The catch:** The "gradually reduce examples" schedule is another hyperparameter to tune. And if your tool interface is complex enough, few-shot examples might not be sufficient to bootstrap.
